title: Public Records Request — Indiana Office of Technology Generative AI Records

communications.subject: Indiana Access to Public Records Act Request: Public Records Request — Indiana Office of Technology Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
Indiana Office of Technology
100 N. Senate Avenue, Room N551
Indianapolis, IN 46204

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Indiana's use of generative AI tools by state employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Google Gemini, "Captain Record," "Ask Indiana," or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of ChatGPT, Google Gemini, "Captain Record," "Ask Indiana," or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

The Indiana Secretary of State's Office launched "Captain Record," a generative AI tool built on Google Cloud's Vertex AI and Gemini 2.0, enabling users to search more than 100 years of state documents using natural language queries (see StateScoop, "Indiana launches 'Captain Record,' the AI tool that searches 100 years of state documents," https://statescoop.com/indiana-captain-record-ai-search-tool/). The state also launched the "Ask Indiana" public-facing AI chatbot on IN.gov (see Indiana Capital Chronicle, "Meet 'Ask Indiana,' state government's new AI chatbot," May 15, 2025, https://indianacapitalchronicle.com/2025/05/15/meet-ask-indiana-state-governments-new-ai-resident-assistant-chatbot/). Additionally, the Secretary of State's Office partnered with Google to launch "Liz" and "Otto," AI chatbots supporting the INBiz platform and auto dealer licensing system, respectively. Indiana's AI Policy and Guidance is published at https://www.in.gov/mph/AI/.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Google Gemini-based tools such as "Captain Record," logs may be available through Google Cloud administrative consoles or server-side conversation storage. For "Ask Indiana," logs may be available through the platform's administration tools. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under Indiana's Access to Public Records Act, IC 5-14-3, public records include "any writing, paper, report, study, map, photograph, book, card, tape recording, or other material that is created, received, retained, maintained, or filed by or with a public agency." IC 5-14-3-2(q). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by state employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Indiana's published AI Policy and Guidance further confirms that these AI tools are part of official state operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

title: FOIA Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: FOIA Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to EOIR's use of generative AI tools. As EOIR's AI procurement may be managed at the DOJ level, we respectfully request that this request also be routed to the DOJ Office of the Chief Information Officer (OCIO) and the Justice Management Division for any responsive records related to AI tools available to EOIR employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, ChatGPT Enterprise, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, and any other generative AI chatbot tools used by EOIR employees (including immigration judges, Board members, law clerks, and administrative staff) — including prompts submitted by employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, or other AI chatbot tools, including any DOJ-level policies applicable to EOIR.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.


We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A)(iii). If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any additional questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,

Dan Bateyko
PhD Student
Cornell University
drb348@cornell.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Federal Records Act, recorded information created or received by a federal agency in connection with public business can qualify as a federal record when it is preserved or appropriate for preservation. 44 U.S.C. § 3301(a)(1)(A). NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely federal records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023). Consistent with that approach, the Army's August 26, 2025 AI compliance memorandum requires Army system owners to capture and manage AI prompts and AI-generated content and to maintain record material needed to support FOIA responses. Army CIO Issues Guidance on AI Compliance With Records Management, FOIA, ExecutiveGov (Sept. 2, 2025).

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Dan Bateyko

title: Request for certain purchase records

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: Request for certain purchase records
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

1. Purchase records, invoices, procurement documents and other documents sufficient to show any and all disbursement of public funds for the acquisition by your agency since July 1, 2023 of any of the following:
• Firearms of .50 caliber or greater
• Patrol rifles (as commonly denominated for law enforcement use) or assault rifles (as defined in Sections 30510 and 30515 of the California Penal Code)
• Projectile launch platforms, such as 40mm projectile launchers, "bean bag" or specialty impact munition ("SIM") weapons, and "riot guns" used to disperse chemical agents
• Explosives and pyrotechnics, such as "flash bang" grenades and explosive breaching tools, 37mm and 40mm 'less lethal' munitions, and chemical weapons such as "teargas", "pepper balls", "OC spray" and related items.

2. Records of deployment by your agency since July 1, 2023 of projectile launch platforms and munitions, such as 40mm projectile launchers, "bean bag" or specialty impact munition ("SIM") weapons, and "riot guns" used to disperse chemical agents; explosives and pyrotechnics, such as "flashbang" grenades and explosive breaching tools; and chemical weapons such as "teargas" and "pepper balls".

3. Records that include a glossary and/or explanation for "Purchasing Group" initials or abbreviations, as used in the CDCR purchases (see column D in "CDCRArmory_Spreadsheet" file of CDCR response to Public Records Act request # M003971-122821 in January 2022).

4. Records that indicate the number and category (e.g. APP, CCCMS, EOP, RHE, ICF-high, ICF-low, etc) of mental health inmates, for each CDCR facility, in 2025. 

Please reference CDCR's responses to Public Records Act requests # M003971-122821 in January 2022 and # C011884-071723 in August 2023. For #1 through #3 above, we request records of the same type and in a similar format as for those requests. 

If you have questions about this request, please don't hesitate to contact me directly via phone, 510-282-8983 or email, jlindsay-poland@afsc.org.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

John Lindsay-Poland

title: CIA: Prince David Peter Abashidze

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: CIA: Prince David Peter Abashidze
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I hereby request copies of all records in the custody or control of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) — and any predecessor agency, including the Office of Strategic Services (OSS) — that relate to, mention, reference, or were generated in connection with the individual described below.

Subject of Request -

Full Name: David Peter Abashidze
Also known as / name variants: Davit Abashidze; D.P. Abashidze; Prince Abashidze; Давид Петрович Абашидзе (Cyrillic)
Title: Prince (Knyaz), of the Georgian noble House of Abashidze
Date of Birth: July 17, 1878
Place of Birth: Tiflis (Tbilisi), Georgia, Imperial Russian Empire
Date of Death: July 11, 1952
Place of Death: New York City, New York, United States
Nationality at Death: Naturalized United States citizen
Business Affiliation: Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation (New York)
Proof of death is enclosed: a copy of the obituary published in The New York Times on July 12, 1952, confirming the subject's death and the above biographical details.
Link to obit: https://www.nytimes.com/1952/07/12/archives/david-p-abasifidzu-a-georgian-prince.html

Records Sought - 

I request all responsive records regardless of format (paper, microfilm, electronic, index cards, or any other medium), including but not limited to:

Any name-indexed files, subject files, or personality files concerning David Peter Abashidze or the Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation;
Any records generated or received during the period 1940–1952 relating to Mr. Abashidze's business dealings with Soviet state entities or Soviet-affiliated trading partners, including cables, memoranda, reports, assessments, or contact reports;
Any records generated or received during the OSS period (1941–1945) referencing Mr. Abashidze, whether in the context of émigré intelligence, Caucasian affairs, or Soviet commercial penetration;
Any records referencing Mr. Abashidze's multilingual capabilities (he was known to speak Georgian, Russian, and English) or his potential utility as a source, contact, or liaison;
Any records relating to the House of Abashidze or Georgian émigré networks in New York City in which David Peter Abashidze is named or can be reasonably identified;
Any index entries, card files, or database entries in the CIA's Central Reference and Records Facility (CRRF) or any predecessor index system referencing the above name variants or the Caucasian-American Mining & Trading Corporation;
Any liaison referrals or records shared with, received from, or jointly generated with the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the State Department, the Office of Naval Intelligence, or Military Intelligence (G-2) concerning the above subject.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware

title: MFCU abuse / neglect referrals and outcomes

communications.subject: Nevada Public Records Act Request: MFCU abuse / neglect referrals and outcomes
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Nevada Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

for the period January 1, 2019 through the date this request is processed: 

1. Aggregate complaint, referral, and case data

Records sufficient to show, by calendar year:

the number of complaints or referrals received by the Medicaid Fraud Control Unit involving alleged patient abuse or neglect;
the number of such complaints or referrals involving community-based, residential, group-home, board-and-care, assisted-living, provider-operated, or other noninstitutional congregate settings;
the source of referral, including but not limited to Adult Protective Services, Nevada Medicaid, the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance, law enforcement, facility/provider self-reports, family members, advocates, or other sources;
the allegation type, including abuse, neglect, exploitation, sexual abuse, physical abuse, emotional abuse, medication-related neglect, restraint, seclusion, unexplained injury, serious bodily injury, hospitalization, death, elopement, theft, or similar categories if tracked;
the number of matters opened for investigation;
the number of matters declined, closed without action, referred elsewhere, resolved civilly, referred for criminal prosecution, or resulting in any other enforcement or administrative outcome;
the number of matters referred by the Attorney General or MFCU to Nevada Medicaid, Adult Protective Services, the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance, local law enforcement, or local prosecutors.

2. Logs, spreadsheets, dashboards, or reports

Any logs, spreadsheets, dashboards, annual reports, quarterly reports, summaries, or other records sufficient to show the information requested in Item 1.

3. Policies, manuals, intake criteria, and jurisdiction guidance

All policies, manuals, written guidance, training materials, intake criteria, routing criteria, or jurisdictional guidance concerning:

when allegations of abuse or neglect fall within MFCU jurisdiction;
how MFCU distinguishes between Medicaid fraud matters and patient abuse/neglect matters;
how MFCU handles complaints involving community placements, residential facilities, provider-operated homes, or other noninstitutional settings;
referral protocols between the Attorney General/MFCU and Adult Protective Services, Nevada Medicaid, the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance, or local law enforcement.

4. Interagency agreements and referral protocols

Any memoranda of understanding, desk procedures, written referral protocols, or similar records governing coordination between the Attorney General or MFCU and:

Adult Protective Services;
Nevada Medicaid;
the Bureau of Health Care Quality and Compliance;
local police or sheriffs’ offices;
district attorneys or local prosecutors.

5. Interagency communications about abuse/neglect in community placements

All emails, email attachments, memoranda, slide decks, meeting agendas, meeting minutes, or internal summaries from January 1, 2021 to present concerning abuse, neglect, exploitation, serious incidents, or referral failures in community-based or residential care settings, where the communication includes any of the following terms:

“group home”
“community placement”
“board and care”
“assisted living”
“HCBS”
“abuse”
“neglect”
“exploitation”
“critical incident”
“restraint”
“seclusion”
“medication error”
“death”
“serious injury”
“referral”
“APS”
“HCQC”
“Nevada Medicaid”

To narrow this request, I am seeking communications between or among the Attorney General’s Office / MFCU and other public agencies, not all internal emails on unrelated matters.

6. Closed case summaries and outcome records

Any de-identified case summaries, closing summaries, synopsis records, or other records sufficient to show the nature and outcome of closed MFCU matters involving allegations of abuse or neglect in community-based or residential settings, excluding personally identifying patient information.

7. Public-facing or releasable enforcement materials

Any press releases, public summaries, case announcements, settlement materials, prosecution summaries, or other releasable records concerning closed abuse/neglect matters involving residential or community care settings during the requested period.

8. Top provider/facility records

Records sufficient to show the providers or facilities most frequently appearing in complaints, referrals, or investigations handled by MFCU concerning abuse or neglect in community-based or residential settings during the requested period, including the total number of appearances for each provider or facility if tracked.

If full records cannot be produced for any item above, please provide records sufficient to show the requested information in aggregate or de-identified form. I am not requesting protected health information or personal identifying information of alleged victims.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

David Cutie

title: Public Records Request — Denver Generative AI Records

communications.subject: Colorado Open Records Act Request: Public Records Request — Denver Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Records Administrator
City and County of Denver
201 W. Colfax Avenue
Denver, CO 80202

Dear Records Administrator,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the City and County of Denver's use of generative AI tools by city employees, including Microsoft Copilot Chat and Ask Dee (AiseraGPT).

Pursuant to the Colorado Open Records Act, C.R.S. § 24-72-203(3)(b), we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from Ask Dee (AiseraGPT), Microsoft Copilot Chat, Sunny AI, ChatGPT, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by City and County of Denver employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing City and County of Denver employee use of Ask Dee, Microsoft Copilot Chat, Sunny AI, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to city employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

The City and County of Denver has deployed Ask Dee, powered by AiseraGPT, which uses domain-specific large language models to automatically resolve employee IT support requests such as software provisioning, account troubleshooting, and hardware issues, auto-resolving 81% of inquiries (see Aisera, "City & County of Denver Customer Story," https://aisera.com/customers/city-and-county-of-denver/). Denver's Chief AI and Information Officer Suma Nallapati has overseen the development of the Sunny AI platform and the city's partnership with Microsoft on Copilot Chat for employee use (see Harvard Data-Smart City Solutions, "Personalizing Government at Scale: Denver's AI Strategy," https://datasmart.hks.harvard.edu/personalizing-denver-ai; see also GovTech, "How a New Title for Denver's CIO Helps Power AI Work," https://www.govtech.com/artificial-intelligence/how-a-new-title-for-denvers-cio-helps-power-ai-work). Denver has also issued an RFP seeking skilled AI vendors to drive innovation and improve the resident experience (see City and County of Denver, "AI RFP Press Release," https://denvergov.org/Government/Agencies-Departments-Offices/Agencies-Departments-Offices-Directory/Technology-Services/News/AI-RFP-Press-Release).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Ask Dee (AiseraGPT) maintains server-side conversation logs and resolution data accessible through the Aisera administrative dashboard. For Microsoft Copilot Chat, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For ChatGPT, conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Colorado has enacted one of the nation's most comprehensive AI records mandates. The Colorado AI Act (SB 24-205, signed 2024, effective February 1, 2026) requires deployers of high-risk AI systems — including government agencies — to implement risk management policies, conduct impact assessments, and maintain documentation and records of AI system use and outcomes. C.R.S. § 6-1-1701 et seq. The Colorado Open Records Act defines "public records" to include all writings made or maintained by any state agency regardless of format. C.R.S. § 24-72-202. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

FOIA Officer
Virginia Information Technologies Agency (VITA)
7325 Beaufont Springs Drive
Richmond, VA 23225

Dear FOIA Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Virginia's use of generative AI tools by state employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from Microsoft Copilot Chat, ChatGPT, Google Gemini, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of Microsoft Copilot Chat, ChatGPT, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Governor Youngkin signed Executive Order 30, and VITA subsequently published the "Utilization of Artificial Intelligence by the Commonwealth of Virginia" policy standard governing AI use across state agencies (see https://www.vita.virginia.gov/media/vitavirginiagov/it-governance/ea/pdf/Utilization-of-Artificial-Intelligence-by-COV-Policy-Standard.pdf). VITA's AI registry has approved 112 AI use cases submitted by state agencies (see Virginia Mercury, "Virginia's artificial intelligence registry faces transparency challenges, study shows," Nov. 12, 2025, https://virginiamercury.com/2025/11/12/virginias-artificial-intelligence-registry-faces-transparency-challenges-study-shows/). Copilot Chat and the AI translation tool Smartling have been made available to all executive branch agencies, with VITA offering virtual training sessions on Copilot Chat for Commonwealth employees (see https://www.vita.virginia.gov/news--events/latest-events/name-1057122-en.html).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Virginia Freedom of Information Act, Va. Code 2.2-3700 et seq., public records include "all writings and recordings that consist of letters, words or numbers, or their equivalent, set down by handwriting, typewriting, printing, photostatting, photography, magnetic impulse, optical or magneto-optical form, mechanical or electronic recording or other form of data compilation, however stored, and regardless of physical form or characteristics, prepared or owned by, or in the possession of a public body or its officers, employees or agents in the transaction of public business." Va. Code 2.2-3701. AI chatbot interaction logs used by state employees in their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Executive Order 30 and VITA's AI policy further confirm that these AI tools are being deployed as part of official state operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that Axon Draft One generates draft police reports from body-worn camera audio; the initial AI-generated drafts and any officer edits may be retained in the Axon Evidence system. I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work. 

We are writing to request records related to San Jose's use of generative AI tools by city employees. 

We are requesting the following records: 

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT Teams, Microsoft Copilot, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by city employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV). 

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing city employee use of ChatGPT Teams, Microsoft Copilot, or other generative AI chatbot tools. 

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data. 

4. Administrative usage statistics for ChatGPT Teams and any other generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed. 

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses). 

San Jose has purchased 89 ChatGPT Teams licenses at $400 per account, spending more than $35,000, for city employees to use generative AI in their work (see Washington Post, "ChatGPT helps write this mayor's speeches. Now he wants a thousand city workers using AI," July 17, 2025, https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2025/07/17/ai-at-work-san-jose-mayor-matt-mahan-chatgpt/2663b12a-6313-11f0-bf70-56d8888ebb94_story.html; see also Fortune, "Liberal mayor proudly outsources part of his job to ChatGPT," July 17, 2025, https://fortune.com/2025/07/17/san-jose-mayor-matt-mahan-uses-chatgpt-ai-to-do-job/). Mayor Matt Mahan has publicly described using ChatGPT for his own work, and by 2026 he aims to have about 15% of the city's nearly 7,000 workers trained to use AI. San Jose has published generative AI guidelines for city employees (see https://www.sanjoseca.gov/your-government/departments-offices/information-technology/itd-generative-ai-guideline) and is a founder of the GovAI Coalition, a network of government agencies promoting responsible AI use (see https://www.sanjoseca.gov/your-government/departments-offices/information-technology/ai-reviews-algorithm-register/govai-coalition).

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20. 

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below. 

Thank you for your time and assistance. 

Sincerely, 
Alexander Spangher 
Postdoctoral Researcher 
Stanford University 
alexspan@stanford.edu 

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT Teams conversation histories can be exported by workspace administrators through the ChatGPT admin console, or by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful. 

P.P.S. Under the California Public Records Act, Cal. Gov. Code 6253(c), public records include "any writing containing information relating to the conduct of the public's business prepared, owned, used, or retained by any state or local agency regardless of physical form or characteristics." Cal. Gov. Code 6252(e). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by city employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records. 

Upload documents directly: https://www.muckrock.com/respond/2266858/ 
Our staff is currently searching for and collecting documents. We will notify you by 4/29/2026 of disclosable public records.  We will, at that time, also inform you of the costs incurred by the City in copying the responsive documents. 
You can monitor the progress of your request at the link below and you'll receive an email when your request has been completed. Again, thank you for using the Public Records Center.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

communications.subject: Pennsylvania Right to Know Act Request: Public Records Request — Pennsylvania Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 9, 2026

Open Records Officer
Commonwealth of Pennsylvania
Office of Administration
613 North Street
Harrisburg, PA 17120

Dear Open Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Pennsylvania's use of generative AI tools by state employees, including ChatGPT Enterprise and Microsoft Copilot Chat.

We are requesting the following records under 65 P.S. § 67.901:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT Enterprise, Microsoft Copilot Chat, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by Commonwealth employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing Commonwealth employee use of ChatGPT Enterprise, Microsoft Copilot Chat, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for ChatGPT Enterprise and Microsoft Copilot Chat showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Pennsylvania conducted a first-in-the-nation pilot program in partnership with OpenAI, in which 175 employees from 14 agencies used ChatGPT Enterprise over the course of a year, reporting an average time savings of 95 minutes per day (see Shapiro Administration, "Lessons from Pennsylvania's Generative AI Pilot with ChatGPT," https://www.pa.gov/content/dam/copapwp-pagov/en/oa/documents/programs/information-technology/documents/openai-pilot-report-2025.pdf; see also City & State PA, "Shapiro says AI has 'real promise' as he unveils pilot program results," Mar. 2025, https://www.cityandstatepa.com/policy/2025/03/shapiro-says-ai-has-real-promise-he-unveils-pilot-program-results/403965/). The administration subsequently expanded access to ChatGPT Enterprise and Microsoft Copilot Chat to all qualified Commonwealth employees, with approximately 2,200 employees now on Copilot. Over 1,300 employees have completed InnovateUS training on safe AI use, with 3,200 more enrolled (see Spotlight PA, "Critics warn of risks as more PA governments turn to AI," Aug. 2025, https://www.spotlightpa.org/news/2025/08/ai-chatgpt-shapiro-pennsylvania-government-environment/; see also PublicSource, "Pennsylvania government to expand employees' use of AI," https://www.publicsource.org/pennsylvania-government-expand-artificial-intelligence-ai/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, "WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents" (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, interaction data may be available through Google Vault or Google Takeout. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Pennsylvania's Right-to-Know Law defines "record" as information regardless of physical form that documents a transaction or activity of an agency. 65 P.S. § 67.102. The Office of Open Records has confirmed that electronic communications on all platforms constitute public records. Governor Shapiro's Executive Order 2024-02 established a Generative AI Governance Board, and the Office of Information Technology issued GenAI guidelines directing state employees to document AI tool usage and treat AI-generated outputs as agency records. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.

Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

Maryland has also piloted Microsoft Copilot for state employee use.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, workspace administrators may access usage logs through the Google Admin console. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Maryland Public Information Act, Md. State Gov't 10-611 et seq., public records include "the original or any copy of any documentary material that is made by a unit or instrumentality of the State government or of a political subdivision, is received by a unit or instrumentality of the State government or of a political subdivision in connection with the transaction of public business, and is in any form." Md. State Gov't 10-611(g)(1). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by state employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Executive Order 01.01.2024.02 and the AI Governance Act of 2024 (SB 818) further establish that AI tools are part of official state government operations subject to oversight and reporting. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

communications.communication:
Subject: FOIA Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 7, 2026

Office of Information Policy
U.S. Department of Justice
441 G Street NW, 6th Floor
Washington, DC 20530
DOJ.OIP.FOIA@usdoj.gov

Dear FOIA Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the Department of Justice's use of generative AI tools by department employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from the OIG internal Azure OpenAI chatbot (DOJ-0195), DEA policy query chatbot (DOJ-0316), FBI policy chatbot (DOJ-0269), ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by DOJ headquarters employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing DOJ employee use of generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.


We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

DOJ's 2025 AI Use Case Inventory documents 315 AI use cases — up from 241 in 2024 and 15 in 2023 — including multiple component-level GenAI chatbots across OIG, DEA, FBI, EOUSA, and BOP (downloadable at https://www.justice.gov/ai/ai-inventory; see also FedScoop, Feb 11, 2026: https://fedscoop.com/justice-department-artificial-intelligence-ai-surveillance-inventory-predictive-technology-algorithm-bias/). The Bureau of Prisons has used ChatGPT to "improve the quality of written communication, emails, and fliers." A department-wide Microsoft Copilot deployment is also underway. Dillon Bergin of MuckRock filed related FOIA requests to OIP in January 2025, and immigration attorney Matthew Hoppock obtained DOJ's internal AI guidance via FOIA.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Claude, export is similar, except via "Settings > Privacy > Export Data". For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Federal Records Act, recorded information created or received by a federal agency in connection with public business can qualify as a federal record when it is preserved or appropriate for preservation. 44 U.S.C. § 3301(a)(1)(A). NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely federal records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02. Consistent with that approach, the Army's August 26, 2025 AI compliance memorandum requires Army system owners to capture and manage AI prompts and AI-generated content and to maintain record material needed to support FOIA responses. See "Army CIO Issues Guidance on AI Compliance With Records Management, FOIA," ExecutiveGov (Sept. 2, 2025), https://www.executivegov.com/articles/army-garciga-application-owners-ai-records-mgmt.

We are writing to request records related to Cook County's use of generative AI and machine learning tools by county employees, including the Assessor's Office automated valuation models.

Pursuant to the Illinois Freedom of Information Act, 5 ILCS 140/3, we are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by Cook County employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. 2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing Cook County employee use of generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed for any generative AI tools deployed to county employees.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

The Cook County Assessor's Office has been a national leader in transparent use of AI and machine learning for government operations. Under Assessor Fritz Kaegi, the office publicly released its residential assessment and modeling code as well as underlying datasets on GitLab and the Cook County Open Data Portal, making Cook County the first assessor's office in the United States known to open-source its automated valuation models (see Cook County Assessor's Office, "Cook County Assessor's Office Publicly Releases Residential Assessment Code and Models," https://www.cookcountyassessoril.gov/news/cook-county-assessors-office-publicly-releases-residential-assessment-code-and-models; see also Route Fifty, "County assessor's code repository powers transparent government," https://gcn.com/cloud-infrastructure/2022/08/county-assessors-code-repository-powers-transparent-government/376220/). The office's model-res-avm uses machine learning trained on property characteristics and sales data to generate assessed values for all residential properties (see GitHub, Cook County Assessor's Office, https://github.com/ccao-data). The Assessor's Office also released its 2025 Open Data Refresh with newly available data assets (see Cook County Open Data, "Assessor — 2025 Open Data Refresh," https://datacatalog.cookcountyil.gov/stories/s/Assessor-2025-Open-Data-Refresh/gzdr-q7c4/).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console or the Purview compliance center. For Google Gemini, administrators can access interaction logs through the Google Workspace Admin Console under Reporting > Audit and Investigation. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Illinois's Freedom of Information Act explicitly enumerates "electronic data processing records, electronic communications, and recorded information of any kind" as public records. 5 ILCS 140/2(c). The Public Access Counselor within the AG's office has issued binding opinions confirming that electronic messages are public records. Governor Pritzker's Executive Order 2024-07 directed the creation of an AI governance framework including documentation requirements. Consistent with this trend nationally, NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely government records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02.
